---
title: "PDB Cloud with an Attached Polymer"
subtitle: "Combine numerical coordinates with an analytic subunit"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

This tutorial parses a deterministic two-atom PDB fragment, creates a
finite-sphere Debye cloud, exposes atom 2 as `attachment`, and connects a
Gaussian polymer. Coordinates and radii use ångström, so q uses inverse
ångström. It assumes the environment from the
[diblock tutorial](diblock-copolymer.qmd).

## Pipeline

```text
PDB text -> atom records -> parameter profile -> DebyeSphereCloud
         -> named atom reference -> GaussianPolymer -> combined structure
```

## Python

### 1. Parse PDB atom records

Import the packages and define a small inline PDB fragment. Keeping the input
inside the tutorial makes the example deterministic and independent of a
network download.

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pyseb

pdb_text = """\
ATOM      1  N   GLY A   1       0.000   0.000   0.000  1.00 20.00           N
ATOM      2  C   GLY A   1       4.000   0.000   0.000  1.00 20.00           C
END
"""

atoms = pyseb.PDBParser().parse_string(pdb_text)
print("parsed atoms:", len(atoms))
```

The parser returns atom records containing serial numbers, elements, and
Cartesian coordinates.

### 2. Convert atoms to a numerical cloud

The parameter profile maps elements to sphere radii and excess scattering
lengths. The build options also expose atom serial 2 as a named reference point
called `attachment`.

```{python}
profile = pyseb.AtomParameterProfile()
profile.set_element("N", radius=1.55, beta=7.0)
profile.set_element("C", radius=1.70, beta=6.0)

options = pyseb.AtomCloudBuildOptions()
options.reference_atom_serials = {"attachment": 2}
cloud = pyseb.AtomCloudBuilder.build(atoms, profile, options)
print("atom scatterers:", cloud.scattererCount())
```

`cloud` is a numerical `DebyeSphereCloud`: its scatterer coordinates and
finite radii are now ready for direct q-dependent evaluation.

### 3. Attach an analytic polymer

Add the cloud as `fragment`, connect a Gaussian polymer to its named atom
reference, and name the combined graph `conjugate`.

```{python}
world = pyseb.World("PDBTutorial")
graph = world.Add(cloud, "fragment")
world.Link("GaussianPolymer", "polymer.end1", "fragment.attachment")
world.Add(graph, "conjugate")
```

This graph mixes numerical coordinate scattering with an analytic Gaussian
polymer in one structure.

### 4. Evaluate both structures

The cloud contains its own numerical scattering parameters, so the standalone
`fragment` takes an empty dictionary. The combined model additionally needs
the polymer's `beta` and `Rg`.

```{python}
parameters = {"beta_polymer": 4.0, "Rg_polymer": 6.0}
q = np.linspace(0.0, 0.5, 150)
fragment = np.asarray(world.EvaluateFormFactor("fragment", {}, q.tolist()))
conjugate = np.asarray(
    world.EvaluateFormFactor("conjugate", parameters, q.tolist())
)

print(f"F_conjugate(0) = {conjugate[0]:.8f}")
```

The combined normalized form factor is exactly one at q = 0.

### 5. Compare the form factors

```{python}
#| label: fig-python-pdb-polymer
#| fig-cap: "Numerical PDB cloud before and after attachment of an analytic polymer."

plt.plot(q, fragment, label="PDB cloud")
plt.plot(q, conjugate, label="PDB cloud + polymer")
plt.xlabel(r"$q\;[\mathrm{\AA}^{-1}]$")
plt.ylabel(r"$F(q)$")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

The difference between the curves contains both the polymer contribution and
its interference with the PDB-derived cloud.

## C++

```cpp
{{< include cpp/pdb-polymer.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from _helpers import run_tutorial

run_tutorial("pdb-polymer")
```

Both versions use the same PDB records, element radii, excess scattering
lengths, named atom reference, and polymer parameters.

## Next steps

- [PDB structures reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/PDB-Structures)
- [Numerical subunits](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Numerical-Subunits)
- [Extended Python and numerical guide](pyseb_tutorial.qmd)
